{"step_by_step_thinking": "Option A states that platelets <40,000 cu/mm is a contraindication. This is supported by the document 'Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Contraindications -- General Contraindications' which mentions coagulation abnormalities (platelet count <100,000/mL, international normalized ratio (INR) >1.5) as a contraindication. Option B states that the procedure can be done in both prone or lateral position. This is supported by the document 'Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Preparation -- Patient Positioning' which discusses the advantages of the prone position and the supine and lateral decubitus positions. Option C states that infiltrative and granulomatous diseases are indications. This is supported by the document 'Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Indications' which mentions that transbronchial biopsy (TBLB) is useful for diffuse and localized pulmonary disorders, including infiltrative and granulomatous diseases. Option D states that no breath holding is needed. However, the document 'Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment -- Additional Procedural Considerations' mentions that the patient should be rolled so that the puncture site is downward against the bed, and this should be done as the patient exhales to mitigate the likelihood of a reflex Valsalva maneuver. This implies that breath holding is necessary to avoid this maneuver.", "answer_choice": "D"}
